claim: Intel N100 mini-PCs idle at 5-8W and peak at 20-25W under load, versus 80-120W idle for tower builds with desktop CPUs and spinning drives, costing £50-£90/year extra in electricity.
quote: A typical Intel N100-based mini PC idles at 5–8W and peaks at 20–25W under load. A tower with a desktop CPU and spinning drives can idle at 80–120W. Over a year, that difference costs you £50–£90 extra in electricity.

claim: Mini-PCs typically have only one or two M.2 slots and no 3.5-inch bays, requiring USB/Thunderbolt enclosures or a paired NAS for bulk storage.
quote: The main trade-off is internal storage expandability—mini PCs typically have one or two M.2 slots and no 3.5-inch bay. For a NAS-heavy setup you will want to attach USB 3.1 or Thunderbolt external enclosures, or pair the mini PC with a dedicated NAS appliance for bulk storage.

claim: ServeTheHome cannot recommend Synology in 2025-2026 due to drive lock-in restricting dedup, lifespan analysis, firmware updates, and pool creation on third-party drives, with Synology-branded drives capped at 16TB.
quote: volume-wide deduplication, lifespan analysis, and automatic firmware updates for third-party devices will be disabled. There are also restrictions on the creation of storage pools.

claim: Synology's drive lock-in for 2025 Plus models excludes Seagate IronWolf and WD Red, with significant community backlash and refused upgrades among longtime customers.
quote: Synology has started locking down drive compatibility in their latest NAS models — most notably, requiring Synology-branded hard drives for full support and performance on models like the DS925+, DS1522+

claim: Synology's DSM remains industry-leading software but hardware pricing has risen with no meaningful upgrades and sometimes apparent downgrades, eroding the platform's value proposition.
quote: what changed during this time is the hardware pricing — and not in a good way. Recent price increases, coupled with no meaningful hardware upgrades, and sometimes even what feel like downgrades, have become hard to ignore.

claim: UGREEN's DXP series in 2025-2026 offers Intel N100/i3/i5 CPUs, built-in NPU, and 10GbE at prices 20-30% lower than comparable Synology models, capitalizing on the drive lock-in controversy.
quote: UGREEN emerged as Synology's most aggressive competitor in 2025-2026, capitalizing on the drive lock-in controversy. Their DXP series offers Intel N100, i3, or i5 processors (vastly superior to Synology's 2018 AMD chip), built-in NPU for AI photo recognition, and 10GbE networking at prices 20-30% lower than comparable Synology models.

claim: The Dell OptiPlex 7070 Micro, HP EliteDesk 800 G5 Mini, and Lenovo M720q form the 2026 used-enterprise tiny-trio sweet spot at roughly $100-150 each in lots, ideal for clusters.
quote: the Dell OptiPlex 7070 Micro, HP EliteDesk 800 G5 Mini, or Lenovo M720q represent the strongest balance between performance, reliability, and long-term usability

claim: ServeTheHome's TinyMiniMicro project established the 1L business mini-PC family (HP EliteDesk/ProDesk Mini, Dell OptiPlex Micro, Lenovo ThinkCentre Tiny) as the canonical homelab hardware tier from corporate refresh fleets.
quote: HP's EliteDesk Mini series are business desktops shrunk to 1-liter form factor and show up on the used market in bulk as companies refresh their fleets, and they're exceptional homelab machines.

claim: An N100/N150 mini-PC delivers ~4x the single-thread performance of a Raspberry Pi 5 with 16GB RAM, NVMe storage, Intel Quick Sync transcoding, and full x86 Docker compatibility for $130-160 — a Pi 5 with case/PSU/SSD lands in the same bracket.
quote: For $130–160, you get 4x the performance with an N100 mini PC, full x86 compatibility (every Docker image works), NVMe storage, and 16 GB RAM.

claim: The Raspberry Pi 5's VideoCore GPU cannot accelerate Plex or Jellyfin transcoding, leaving its 4 ARM cores at 2.4GHz unable to keep up with even one 1080p software transcode; PCIe Gen 2 x1 also bottlenecks NVMe and NICs.
quote: The Pi's VideoCore GPU cannot be used for Plex or Jellyfin hardware transcoding—if a client requests a transcode, the CPU handles it, and 4 ARM cores at 2.4 GHz cannot keep up with even one 1080p software transcode smoothly.

claim: The 2026 DIY NAS reference build uses a Topton N22 mini-ITX board with Intel Core 3 N355, 8x SATA, 2.5GbE, and a PCIe x1 slot — a budget Topton N100 build with 16GB DDR5 and 4x4TB IronWolf totals around $535 but idles at ~67W.
quote: The 2026 DIY NAS Edition uses the Topton N22 motherboard with Intel Core 3 N355 CPU. The motherboard is similar to last year's Topton N18 but has incrementally more compelling features, particularly the extra 2 SATA ports, and the PCI-e x1 slot.

claim: Topton-style generic ITX NAS boards are viewed as a reliability black box with no clear warranty path, raising community anxiety about running critical infrastructure on them despite generous SATA/2.5GbE port counts.
quote: Topton boards offer an impressive number of ports (8x SATA, multiple 2.5GbE), but they are often viewed as a "black box" regarding reliability, and community members expressed anxiety over building critical infrastructure on generic hardware with no clear warranty path.

claim: Among Chinese mini-PC vendors, Minisforum leads on enthusiast specs and community engagement, Beelink offers proven mid-range value (with inconsistent QC), and GMKtec/AOOSTAR push lowest prices but minimal support, suiting self-troubleshooting buyers.
quote: If you want the most specs for the least money and are comfortable self-troubleshooting, GMKtec or AOOSTAR may offer better value.

claim: The Minisforum MS-01 is treated as the homelab high-end mini-server, with dual 10GbE SFP+, dual 2.5GbE, three M.2 slots, and a 2.5" drive bay — closer to a mini server than a mini PC.
quote: The MinisForum MS-01 has dual 10GbE SFP+, dual 2.5GbE, three M.2 slots, and space for a 2.5" drive. It's a mini server, not just a mini PC.

claim: DIY NAS builds in 2026 are pursued specifically for ECC memory, multiple NVMe slots, 10GbE+ networking, GPU acceleration, and 8+ 3.5-inch drive bays — features that prebuilt NAS and mini-PC categories cannot match.
quote: DIY NAS builds have become extremely popular because users want ECC memory, multiple NVMe slots, higher network speeds, GPU acceleration, and up to eight or more 3.5 inch drives.

claim: Intel N100 mini PCs idle at 6-8W headless and N150 at 7-9W measured at the wall with a Kill-A-Watt meter across multiple Beelink, GMKtec, and Trigkey models.
quote: Beelink Mini S12 Pro (N100) Headless server: 6-8W ... N100 idle (headless): 6-8W; N150 idle (headless): 7-9W; Difference: +1W

claim: Wall-meter measurements show N100 mini PCs at 6-7W idle, N150 at 7W, N305 at 10W, and Ryzen 5560U/5800H/7940HS mini PCs at 12-18W idle.
quote: Beelink S12 Pro: 6W ... Beelink EQ14: 7W idle ... Beelink EQ12 Pro: 10W idle ... Ryzen 5 5560U (Beelink SER5 Pro): 12W idle ... Ryzen 7 5800H (Beelink SER5 Max): 18W idle

claim: ServeTheHome measured the fanless Intel i3-N305 2.5GbE firewall idling at 9-11W under Proxmox VE 8, with sustained-load draw of 28-29W.
quote: Idle was in the 9-11W range in Proxmox VE 8.

claim: A DIY Intel i5-12400 build on an ASUS Prime H770-Plus D4 reached 7W idle at the wall (display off, powertop tuned) before storage was added; six 3.5-inch HDDs in standby contributed roughly 0.8W each.
quote: Idle Power Consumption: 7 watts at wall (display off, with powertop auto-tune) ... 21 watts with 6x 3.5" HDDs in standby (0.8w each)

claim: ServeTheHome measured the Lenovo ThinkCentre M90q Tiny Gen2 idling at 12-14W on 120V power, slightly above the typical 9-12W expected for the TinyMiniMicro class.
quote: idle power consumption on 120V power was around 12-14W. The expectation for these nodes is typically 9-12W idle

claim: An HP EliteDesk 705 G4 Mini (AMD) measured around 11W idle on Windows 11 but climbed to 18W on Debian 12, illustrating OS-level Ryzen idle penalties.
quote: around 11 Watts idle power consumption running Windows 11, though after installing Debian 12 the system used 18 Watts at idle

claim: Raspberry Pi 5 idles around 2.7W with the older C1 stepping and the newer 2GB D0 stepping cuts idle power by roughly 30%, putting it near 2.0W.
quote: the newer 2GB Pi 5 with the D0 chip stepping has almost 30% better idle power draw compared to the older C1 stepping

claim: The Radxa Rock 5B (RK3588) idles at roughly 1.7W with Ethernet and eMMC only, rising to about 8W under Ubuntu and 10W with NVMe plus a USB-C dock attached.
quote: 1.7 watts at idle is quite impressive, albeit it's with Ethernet and eMMC only ... idle power consumption is 10.1W with the internal 256GB NVMe SSD

claim: Seagate's IronWolf Pro 20TB datasheet specifies 5.5W average idle (spinning), 7.7W active, and 1.0W standby/sleep per drive.
quote: 1W standby, 1W sleep, 5.5W average idle and 7.7W average active

claim: WD Red 14TB drives are rated at 3.0W idle while spinning and 0.8W in standby, meaningfully lower than equivalent IronWolf models.
quote: WD Red WD140EFFX 14TB has a rated power consumption of 6.5W under load, 3.0W idling, and 0.8W standby

claim: Consumer NVMe SSDs in homelab/ZFS use typically draw 0.5-1W idle when ASPM/APST work, but FreeBSD and some BIOSes leave drives in higher states drawing several watts each.
quote: on Linux, a Solidigm P44 PRO 2TB SSD consumes less than 1W in addition, while on FreeBSD, the same machine consumes about 4W

claim: ZFS pools resist effective HDD spindown because transaction groups close every 5-30 seconds and metadata writes touch every disk, so spinning-pool savings are usually limited to truly idle workloads.
quote: ZFS is not very idle by default—it closes a transaction group every 5 to 30 seconds, which requires synchronous writes of metadata to disk

claim: An ASRock N100DC-ITX home-server build achieves 6-8W idle at the wall, matching mini-PC class N100 figures despite being a DIY mini-ITX system.
quote: N100DC-ITX (a related model) uses about 6-8W at idle for a home server

claim: UGREEN DXP4800 Plus is a 4-bay NAS with Intel Pentium Gold 8505 (5-core), one 10GbE + one 2.5GbE port, 4 SATA + 2 M.2 NVMe bays, max 144TB.
quote: Intel Pentium Gold 8505 ... 10GbE + 2.5GbE ... 4 SATA drive bays plus 2 M.2 NVMe slots ... maximum storage capacity of 144TB

claim: UGREEN DXP6800 Pro ships with Intel i5-1235U, 8GB DDR5 (expandable to 64GB), dual 10GbE, 6 SATA + 2 M.2 NVMe, priced about $1,028.
quote: Intel i5 1235u processor, 8GB DDR5 memory (expandable to 64GB), dual 10GbE ports ... six SATA and two M.2 NVMe drive bays ... up to 196TB

claim: UGREEN DXP8800 Plus offers 8 SATA + 2 M.2 NVMe bays (max 272TB), Intel i5-1235U, dual 10GbE, list around $1,283.
quote: DXP8800 Plus: 10 drive bays (8 SATA + 2 M.2 NVMe slots) with maximum storage capacity of 272TB ... Intel i5 1235u processor ... dual 10GbE ports

claim: UGREEN NAS hardware ships with a 2-year manufacturer warranty.
quote: UGREEN NAS products come with a 2-year warranty

claim: DXP4800 Plus measured ~24W idle on 2.5GbE rising to ~30W on 10GbE in TechPowerUp/KitGuru testing with WD Red drives.
quote: averaged 24.25W in idle mode with a 2.5GbE connection rising to 30.45W using 10GbE

claim: UGREEN NASync hardware can run TrueNAS, Unraid or Proxmox bare metal once Ugreen's BIOS Watchdog service is disabled.
quote: To boot your own software, you'll need to disable Ugreen's Watchdog service in the BIOS

claim: Community report confirms Proxmox runs successfully on a DXP4800 Plus replacing UGOS.
quote: the Ugreen 4800DXP Plus NAS is now running Proxmox successfully

claim: Aoostar WTR Max is an 11-bay (6 SATA + 5 NVMe) NAS with AMD Ryzen 7 PRO 8845HS, dual 10GbE SFP+, OCuLink and USB4.
quote: AMD Ryzen 7 PRO 8845HS ... six SATA bays and five M.2 NVMe slots ... dual 10GbE SFP+ ports, an OCuLink expansion port, and USB4

claim: Aoostar WTR Max launched at $699 with promo pricing as low as $649 for early buyers.
quote: AOOSTAR WTR MAX(11bay) is about to meet you all! Starting at $699

claim: Aoostar WTR Pro is a 4-bay AMD Ryzen 7 5825U mini-NAS launched at $399, outperforming N100 alternatives 2-3x in CPU work.
quote: AMD Ryzen 7 5825U (8 cores/16 threads, up to 4.5GHz) in a compact 4-bay chassis priced at $399

claim: TrueNAS community discusses Aoostar WTR Max as a viable bring-your-own-OS platform for ZFS, with caveats around firmware quality.
quote: Thoughts on the Aoostar WTR Max for TrueNAS

claim: TerraMaster F4-424 Pro pairs an Intel Core i3 N305 (8-core) with up to 32GB DDR5, 4 SATA + 2 M.2 NVMe bays and dual 2.5GbE.
quote: Intel i3 N300 CPU, 32GB DDR5 memory, and 2x M.2 NVMe SSD bays

claim: F4-424 Pro retails for $450-$550 in early 2026 but lacks 10GbE, which is its main drawback against newer rivals.
quote: January 2026, the TerraMaster F4-424 Pro can typically be found in a price range of $450 to $550 ... The absence of 10GbE and memory limitations are notable drawbacks

claim: TerraMaster F8 SSD Plus packs 8 M.2 NVMe slots, Intel i3-N305, 16GB DDR5 and a 10GbE port in a palm-sized all-flash chassis.
quote: eight M.2 NVMe slots, an Intel Core i3-N305 eight-core processor, 16GB of DDR5 RAM, and a 10GbE network port

claim: TerraMaster offers a 2-year brand-new replacement warranty on its NAS hardware.
quote: exclusive worry-free warranty solution with brand new replacement for 2 years

claim: Minisforum N5 Pro is a 5-bay NAS with AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX PRO 370 (12C/24T Zen 5), up to 96GB DDR5 ECC, 5 SATA + 3 M.2 NVMe, 10GbE+5GbE, OCuLink, USB4.
quote: AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX PRO 370, Zen 5 Architecture, with 12 Cores and 24 Threads ... DDR5 ECC memory support, up to 96GB ... 5-Bay ... up to 144TB

claim: Minisforum N5 starts at $645 (16GB/128GB) while N5 Pro barebone is $1,019 in 2026 pricing.
quote: N5 now starts at $645 with 16 GB of RAM and 128 GB of storage, and the N5 Pro barebone unit is priced at $1,019

claim: ServeTheHome measured the N5 Pro at 32-34W idle in standard config, rising to ~80W under heavy combined CPU/storage/10GbE load.
quote: Idle power consumption was around 32–34W ... Power draw increased to roughly 80W under combined heavy CPU, storage, and 10GbE load

claim: Minisforum MS-A2 mini workstation uses AMD Ryzen 9 9955HX (16C/32T Zen 5, 5.4GHz boost), up to 96GB DDR5-5600, triple PCIe 4.0 M.2/U.2, dual 10GbE SFP+ and PCIe x16 slot.
quote: AMD Ryzen 9 9955HX(16C/32T, up to 5.4GHz) ... 2x2.5G LAN Port|10G SFP+ Port

claim: MS-A2 is positioned as an MS-01 successor with US barebone pricing from $799 and reviewer-tagged 'NAS-suitable' thanks to triple M.2 + dual 10GbE SFP+.
quote: Official US pricing for the MS-A2 with the Ryzen 9 9955HX starts at $799 with no RAM or storage

claim: Beelink ME mini is a 6-NVMe (Gen3 x2 + 5x Gen3 x1) all-flash mini NAS with Intel N150, 12GB LPDDR5, dual 2.5GbE, Wi-Fi 6, in a 99mm cube with internal PSU.
quote: Intel Twin Lake N150 CPU and 12GB of LPDDR5 memory ... up to six M.2 NVMe SSDs—one at Gen 3 x2 and five at Gen 3 x1 ... 99mm cube

claim: Beelink ME mini idles around 7-10W and street-prices around $209-$329 making it the lowest-power option in this shortlist.
quote: as low as 6.9W idle ... currently available in the US at the discounted price of $209 (RRP is $329)

claim: Beelink expanded the ME line for 2026 with ME Mini Pro, ME S, ME X and ME Max variants targeting tiered NAS use cases.
quote: Beelink ME Mini Pro, ME S, ME X and ME Max NAS Series for 2026

claim: Asustor Flashstor 12 Pro FS6712X provides 12 M.2 NVMe slots, Intel Celeron N5105 (4-core), 4GB DDR4 (expandable), one 10GBase-T port and HDMI 2.0.
quote: Quad-Core Intel Celeron N5105 processor, 4GB of DDR4 RAM, 10 GbE LAN ... 12-bay 100% Flash NAS

claim: Asustor Flashstor 12 Pro FS6712X has an MSRP near $799 on Amazon as of April 2026.
quote: Asustor Flashstor 12 Pro FS6712X - 12 Bay Pure M.2 SSD NAS

claim: Asustor Flashstor 6 Gen 2 (FS6806X) upgrades to AMD Ryzen V3C14, 8GB DDR5, Gen4 NVMe, 10GbE and dual USB4 but doubles the price to ~$999.
quote: AMD Ryzen V3C14 ... 8GB DDR5-4800 ... 1 x 10GbE with 2 x USB 4.0 ... double the price

claim: Community sentiment puts TerraMaster as the more established hardware choice but flags TOS software polish concerns versus newer UGREEN UGOS Pro.
quote: Terramaster TOS7 is a copy of Synology with one big difference – their software does not work 50% of the time

claim: Independent review concludes the Aoostar WTR Max is 'almost perfect' for self-hosters running TrueNAS/Proxmox but lacks vendor OS support.
quote: Aoostar WTR Max Review: The Almost Perfect NAS Mini PC

claim: Jonsbo N5 supports up to 12 HDDs, 4 SSDs, ATX/E-ATX motherboards and ATX PSU; N4 hybrid steel/wood is smaller; N3 fits 8x3.5 + 2x2.5 with mini-ITX and SFX PSU; N2 is the smallest and most efficient option for low-energy NAS builds
quote: the N2 stands out as the smallest and most efficient option, perfect for low-energy NAS builds where space is at a premium

claim: Jonsbo N5 is sold on Newegg for $249.99 (down from $469.99) with 12 hot-swap 3.5" bays, 4 side-mount SSDs, supports E-ATX motherboards up to 330mm and ATX PSU
quote: 12x3.5' hotswap HDD disk front installation and 4x2.5' SSD which is Side installation

claim: Jonsbo N3 is a mini-ITX 8+1 bay NAS case requiring an SFX power supply (≤105mm) and supporting up to 130mm CPU coolers
quote: JONSBO N3 Mini-ITX NAS PC Chassis, ITX Computer Case, 8HHD+1-SSDD isk Bays NAS Mini Aluminum with Steel Plate Case, Built-in 2x10cm Fan, Power Support: SFX105mm, Support 130mm CPU Cooler

claim: Topton/CWWK i3-N305 boards offer 8 cores up to 3.8GHz with 15W TDP versus N100's 4 cores at 6W TDP, with the N305 better suited for Plex transcoding and virtualization
quote: The N305 offers double the core count and threads, making it better suited for multitasking and heavier workloads, such as Plex transcoding and running virtualized environments

claim: CWWK 6-bay N100/N305 mini-ITX NAS board is priced at $135.99 (from $226.25) with 6x SATA 3.0, 2x M.2 NVMe, 4x i226-V 2.5GbE NICs, DDR5 and a single PCIe x1 slot
quote: 12th i3-N305 N100 NAS Motherboard 6-Bay DC Power 2xM.2 NVMe 6xSATA3.0 PCIE X1 4x i226-V 2.5G LAN DDR5 ITX Mainboard

claim: CWWK i3-N305 board users report concerns about the soldered CPU and bad stock cooler, while running Proxmox/TrueNAS in a Jonsbo N2 worked well with quiet fan operation
quote: The Alder Lake N100 boards from Topton or CWWK come with a terrible built-in cooler, and since the CPU is soldered, you basically can't replace it

claim: ASRock N100DC-ITX is a mini-ITX board with soldered Intel N100, DC-in jack (19V), 2x SATA3, 1x M.2 NVMe, 1x PCIe 3.0 x4 (x2 mode), and was launched at $129.99
quote: ASRock N100DC-ITX Mini-ITX Motherboard - Intel N100 Quad-Core (3.4GHz), DDR4, HDMI/D-Sub, Dual M.2 (NVMe/WiFi), SATA3, USB 3.2, Gigabit LAN, 19V DC Power

claim: ASRock launched a sister N100M micro-ATX board with a standard 24-pin ATX connector, alongside the N100DC-ITX, both using soldered Intel Alder Lake-N N100 CPUs
quote: ASRock Adopts Alder Lake-N with N100 Based N100DC-ITX and N100M Motherboards

claim: ASRock Rack X570D4I-2T is a mini-ITX server board for AM4 Ryzen with dual 10GbE; current pricing around $451 makes it the premium ECC mini-ITX choice for AMD homelab builds
quote: AsRock Rack X570D4I-2T Mini-ITX Server Motherboard AM4 PGA 1331 X570 AMD Ryzen 3rd Generation Series Processors

claim: ASRock Rack B650D4U is a micro-ATX server board for AM5 Ryzen 7000/8000/9000 and EPYC 4004/4005 with PCIe 5.0 and dual 1GbE, priced around $264; B650D4U-2L2T/BCM with dual 10GbE runs ~$369
quote: AsRock Rack B650D4U Micro-ATX Server Motherboard Single Socket AMD EPYC™ 4005/4004 and AMD Ryzen™ 9000/8000/7000 series Processors (LGA 1718) B650E PCIe 5.0 Dual 1G Lan

claim: SilverStone CS381 is a micro-ATX 8-bay hot-swap NAS chassis with two SFF-8643 backplane connectors (SAS3/SATA), 4 internal 2.5" SSD bays, lockable front door, and SFX/SFX-L PSU support; priced ~$400
quote: a mATX motherboard, 8x hot-swap bays, and has 4x internal bays for SSDs

claim: Fractal Design Node 304 is a 19.2L mini-ITX/mini-DTX case with 6x 3.5/2.5 drive positions, full ATX PSU support up to 160mm, three pre-installed Silent R2 fans, dimensions 250×210×374 mm
quote: 6 3.5"/2.5" HDD/SSD positions ... ATX (up to 160 mm in length) ... 250 x 210 x 374 mm

claim: Fractal Design Node 804 micro-ATX cube case priced around $165 in 2026 with 10x internal 3.5" bays, 2x 2.5" bays, 5 expansion slots — a large alternative to the Jonsbo N3 for mATX builds
quote: 12 x Bay - 3 x 4.72" x Fan(s) Installed - Micro ATX, Mini ITX Motherboard Supported ... 10 x Internal 3.5" Bay - 2 x Internal 2.5" Bay

claim: Sliger CL520 is a USA-made console-style mini-ITX case at $149 retail; it supports M.2 storage only with no 2.5"/3.5" HDD bays, making it unsuitable for HDD-heavy NAS but viable for compact all-flash builds
quote: Sliger Designs is a manufacturing company based in the United States specializing in computer cases and systems

claim: A Ryzen 5600G NAS averages around 25-30W excluding disks, climbing to ~50W with 4x3.5" disks and NVMe drives where disks alone account for ~28W of that draw
quote: averages around 25 to 30 watts ... with four 3.5" disks and NVMe drives ... has an average of a little over 50 watts

claim: PicoPSUs are most efficient at <40-50W loads but become inadequate when 2.5/10GbE NICs and HDD spin-up surge are added; Flex-ATX is the recommended NAS PSU upgrade for higher loads
quote: your NICs will draw too much power on top of your system for the pico PSU to handle

claim: C-States cannot be set in BIOS on some early CWWK N100/N305 boards, leaving idle draw at ~20W with one HDD attached — a real-world gotcha for the budget NAS builder choosing this platform
quote: C-States can't be set in the BIOS, resulting in continuous power draw of 20W with just one 3.5" HDD

claim: A complete Jonsbo N3 + CWWK N305 + 32GB DDR5 + TrueNAS SCALE diskless build totals roughly $650-770, demonstrating the entry price for a modern 8-bay DIY NAS in 2026
quote: build a Jonsbo N3-based NAS with a CWWK Intel N305 motherboard, 32 GB DDR5, and TrueNAS SCALE for a total cost of approximately $650-770 diskless

claim: ZFS co-creator Matt Ahrens stated ZFS does not require ECC RAM more than any other filesystem, while still recommending ECC plus a checksumming filesystem for those who care about data integrity.
quote: There's nothing special about ZFS that requires/encourages the use of ECC RAM more so than any other filesystem. ... if you love your data, use ECC RAM. Additionally, use a filesystem that checksums your data, such as ZFS.

claim: Jim Salter debunks the 'scrub of death' myth, concluding ZFS without ECC poses no special danger and will mitigate damage from bad RAM rather than amplify it.
quote: ZFS will mitigate the damage, not amplify it ... For corrupted data to overwrite good data, ZFS would need to flip bits in corrupted blocks such that they match their checksums—a 1 in 2^256 chance with default SHA validation.

claim: The ASRock Rack EC266D2I is a mini-ITX board for Intel Xeon E-2400 series with DDR5 ECC UDIMM, PCIe 5.0 x16, dual 1GbE and onboard IPMI, targeting compact homelab/NAS builds.
quote: ASRock Rack EC266D2I Mini-ITX 2x 1GLan Single Socket (LGA 1700) Intel Xeon E-2400 series ... DDR5 ECC memory, PCIe 5.0 x16, and remote management via IPMI.

claim: Intel Xeon E-2400 'Raptor Lake' brings DDR5-4800 ECC and PCIe Gen5 to entry servers, following Intel's pattern of taking desktop parts, validating ECC, and selling them for the entry-server segment.
quote: support for DDR5-4800 memory and PCIe Gen5 ... taking what are essentially desktop parts, adding ECC support, and validating them for servers.

claim: AMD removed ECC from official Ryzen 7000 AM5 specs at launch, but ECC works in practice on ASRock and select boards via AGESA firmware, leaving non-PRO Ryzen ECC support unofficial-but-functional in 2026.
quote: When the AMD Ryzen 7000 'Raphael' CPUs were launched along with the brand new Socket AM5, all mention of ECC support was gone.

claim: AMD confirmed Ryzen 8000G APUs do not support ECC RAM despite initial claims, meaning APU buyers wanting ECC must use the OEM-only PRO variants.
quote: AMD confirms Ryzen 8000G APUs don't support ECC RAM, despite initial claims ... AMD has made an arbitrary choice to remove ECC functionality from non-PRO APUs.

claim: Intel N97 and N305 SoCs support In-Band ECC, allowing error correction without workstation-class hardware, though it requires dual-channel soldered LPDDR5 (not single-channel SO-DIMM) to function.
quote: The Intel N-series chips (specifically the N97 and N305) often support In-Band ECC ... A single channel DDR5 SO-DIMM cannot implement the In-Band ECC function because the data bus width cannot be configured dynamically.

claim: PiKVM V4 delivers 1920x1200 @ 60Hz video at 35-50ms latency with ATX power control, virtual mass storage for OS install, and native Tailscale/WireGuard, marketed as industrial-grade with clients including Netflix, AMD and Broadcom.
quote: 1920x1200 @ 60Hz video ... lowest 35-50ms latency ... Power on/off, reboot ... Emulate bootable media ... Access BIOS, reinstall the OS ... Industrial grade and certification

claim: JetKVM launched at $69 on Kickstarter (Dec 2024, shipping April 2025), delivering 1080p60 H.264 video at 30-60ms latency with USB HID emulation, optional WebRTC cloud access, and an RJ12 extension port for ATX power and serial-console add-ons.
quote: JetKVM - A $69 KVM over IP solution with open-source software ... 1080p60 video with 30-60ms latency using H.264 encoding ... RJ11 port supporting hardware extensions like sensors, ATX power control, or serial console access.

claim: The 2025-2026 Practical ZFS / OpenZFS community consensus is 'use ECC if your platform supports it; do not avoid ZFS if it does not' — ZFS without ECC is still better than ext4/NTFS without ECC.
quote: Use ECC if your platform supports it. Don't avoid ZFS if your platform does not. ZFS without ECC is still vastly better than ext4 or NTFS without ECC.

claim: UGREEN DXP8800 Plus has 8 SATA bays plus 2 M.2 NVMe slots and a half-height PCIe 4.0 x4 expansion slot intended primarily for NIC upgrades
quote: UGREEN 8-Bay NAS Server | Massive 256TB Storage & 10GbE Ports

claim: UGREEN DXP480T Plus is an all-flash NAS with 4 M.2 NVMe slots
quote: 4-Slot M.2 NVMe NAS with 32TB | 30-Day Trial

claim: Aoostar WTR Max is a 6+5 bay NAS barebone with 6 SATA bays plus 5 M.2 NVMe slots, ECC RAM support, dual 10GbE SFP+, and USB4/Oculink ports
quote: AOOSTAR WTR MAX AMD R7 8845HS 6+5 Bays Desktop NAS(Barebone),2 * 10GB SFP+ LAN Ports，USB4&Oculink Port，Support ECC RAM, 2*DDR5 5600Mhz Slots，5* M.2 NVME Slots

claim: Aoostar WTR Pro is a 4-bay NAS mini PC with 2 M.2 NVMe slots supporting up to 4x 22TB SATA drives

claim: LSI SAS 9300-8i HBA delivers 12Gb/s SAS / 6Gb/s SATA across 8 internal ports on PCIe 3.0 x8, supporting up to 1024 attached devices via expanders, and is the workhorse HBA for FreeNAS/TrueNAS/Unraid builds
quote: Users can connect up to 1024 SAS and SATA devices with its 8 external and internal ports as well as providing up to 12Gb/s SAS and up to 6Gb/s SATA performance

claim: ServeTheHome recommends LSI 9300-series HBAs as their top pick for napp-it/OmniOS and ZFS NAS builds when scaling beyond motherboard SATA
quote: Top Picks for napp-it and OmniOS HBAs (Host Bus Adapters)

claim: TrueNAS community guidance is explicit that USB-attached drives should not be used for ZFS data pool disks because of unreliable command transport, lack of SMART pass-through, and pool faulting on transient disconnects
quote: Why you should avoid USB attached drives for data pool disks

claim: OpenZFS users on AMD Zen/Zen2 systems report frequent I/O and checksum errors when using UAS-attached DAS, while the same enclosures behave on Intel — making USB DAS a known-bad combination for ZFS on AMD platforms like the Aoostar WTR Max
quote: ZFS + UAS + AMD (ZEN) = I/O Errors

claim: Temporary USB issues can cause ZFS to lock up the entire pool until forced reboot and have caused data loss in reported regressions
quote: Regression: Temporary USB issues cause ZFS lockup until forced reboot, and sometimes data loss!

claim: TerraMaster D5-300 is a 5-bay USB 3.1 Gen1 Type-C DAS supporting RAID 0/1/5/10/JBOD with hardware RAID up to ~100TB
quote: TERRAMASTER D5-300 USB3.1 (Gen1) Type C 5-Bay External Hard Drive Enclosure Support RAID 5, 0, 1, 10, Clone, JBOD, Single Disk Hard Disk RAID Storage

claim: Backblaze 2025 fleet annualized failure rate fell to 1.36% (from 1.55% in 2024) with Q4 2025 hitting 1.13%, and 20TB+ drives now make up nearly 23% of their fleet, indicating high-capacity HDDs are healthy in production
quote: The 2025 yearly annualized failure rate (AFR) was 1.36%, down from 1.55% in 2024 ... 20TB+ models now account for nearly 23% of active drives

claim: The Seagate ST24000NM002H 24TB drive posted a 1.11% AFR in Q1 2025 (8 failures) — its first failures, but acceptable for a young drive on the leading edge of the bathtub curve
quote: The 24TB Seagate had eight failures for Q1 2025 and put up a respectable 1.11% AFR

claim: RAID rebuild times scale with drive capacity since sequential write speeds have not kept pace with capacity growth — a 24TB drive rebuild can take 2–3 days, dramatically increasing exposure to a second failure or URE during the rebuild window
quote: Since sequential write speeds of hard disks have not increased as fast as the capacity, the time to recover from hard disk failure on RAID arrays has become huge

claim: IBM guidance recommends RAID-6 over RAID-5 for large slow drives because the URE probability during a rebuild from a single-parity array becomes unacceptable as drives scale into the 20TB+ range
quote: Re-Evaluating RAID-5 and RAID-6 for slower larger drives

claim: In 2026 a 6-bay desktop NAS in RAID 5 with 24TB drives can deliver well over 100TB usable, making 4–6 bay platforms the practical sweet spot for home users before scale-out becomes necessary
quote: A six-bay desktop NAS in RAID 5 can deliver over 100TB of usable storage — more than most individuals or small businesses will ever need

claim: The DXP8800 Plus PCIe slot accessibility is awkward — adding any expansion card requires partial disassembly of the chassis, limiting practical use of HBAs in UGREEN platforms
quote: The DXP6800 Pro requires some light teardown in order to add a PCIe NIC, or GPU. The PCIe add-on slot is difficult to access
